title: execute_exit_worktree status check uses string equality on git output instead of git plumbing
description:
## Severity
Minor

## Dimension
KISS / Decoupling

## Location
src/tools/worktree.rs:186-192 (git-dir vs git-common-dir comparison)

## Violation
```rust
if git_dir == common_dir || git_dir == ".git" {
    return ("Not in an isolated worktree. ...", true);
}
```

The check determines "am I in the main worktree or a child worktree" by comparing the string outputs of two separate git rev-parse invocations. The two values can drift in any of: trailing whitespace, relative-vs-absolute paths, case (Windows), the special `.git` literal value. Hardcoding `git_dir == ".git"` as a second equality check shows the author hit this edge but only patched the specific instance they encountered.

Cleaner: `git rev-parse --git-common-dir` and `git rev-parse --is-inside-worktree` return canonical values, but git has a dedicated `git worktree list --porcelain` that lists every worktree and marks the main one explicitly. Use that.

Worse, the git_dir extraction (line 181-184) silently defaults to empty string on error: `.unwrap_or_default()`. If git rev-parse fails entirely (corrupted .git), git_dir == "" and common_dir != "" so the function FALSELY claims we are in an isolated worktree and proceeds to remove it.

## Mandated refactor
Use `git worktree list --porcelain`, parse the entries, find the one matching the current cwd, check its position in the list (main is first). This is a single git invocation and gives canonical answers. Drop the dual rev-parse comparison entirely.

## Filed from QA review dispatch
